label: Classical Sāṃkhya

scope.school: Classical Sāṃkhya as codified in Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā
scope.period: c. 350 CE (Sāṃkhyakārikā), with older roots in the epics and Upaniṣads
limitations: Draft awaiting human domain review (D2-001); review needed before any scholarly authority is claimed.
Single-source draft: the SEP has no dedicated Sāṃkhya entry, so all claims currently rest on the IEP entry; a primary-text source (Sāṃkhyakārikā) should be added at review.
Proto-Sāṃkhya of the epics and the theistic Yoga school's adaptation are out of scope.
No claims are recorded for cosmic_structure, time, or human_place as separate primitives: the source treats these within the evolution of the tattvas. Absence means unspecified, not denial.

claims.claim: Reality is an irreducible dualism: 'the two types of entities of Sāṅkhya are Prakṛti and puruṣa-s, namely Nature and persons' — unconscious productive nature and conscious, inactive selves.
The classical system posits no God: 'it is a strong Indian example of metaphysical dualism, but unlike many Western counterparts it is atheistic'.
claims.notes: Atheism holds for the classical system but not the wider tradition: the allied Yoga school 'absorbs the basic dualism of Sāṅkhya, but is theistic, and thus regards one puruṣa as a special puruṣa, called the Lord (Īśvara)', while the later Sāṅkhya-Sūtra 'is markedly atheistic and makes arguments against the existence of God'.

claims.claim: Nature is composed of three strands: 'Prakṛti, or Nature, is comprised of three guṇa-s or qualities. The highest of the three is sattva (essence), the principle of light, goodness and intelligence'.

The manifest world evolves out of unmanifest nature: 'the actual order of evolution is as follows: from root-nature first appears intellect (buddhi); from it, ego (ahaṁkāra); from it the eleven powers'.
Causation follows satkāryavāda: 'the effect already exists in its cause prior to its production... the theory states that there is nothing absolutely new in the product'.
Conscious selves are many and distinct: 'as puruṣa is essentially private for every sentient being, being their true self, there are many irreducibly distinct puruṣa-s'.
Existence in nature is marked by pervasive suffering: 'the first premise of Sāṅkhya is the universal fact of suffering'.
Release comes through discriminative knowledge: 'an escape from this endless circle is possible only through the realization of the fundamental difference between Nature and persons'.
The goal is the isolation of consciousness from nature: 'liberation of puruṣa, in Sāṅkhya, normally called kaivalya (isolation)... comes about through loosening the bond between puruṣa and Prakṛti'.
Knowledge has exactly three sources: 'Sāṅkhya recognizes only three valid sources of information: perception, inference and reliable tradition'.

distinguishing_claims: A strict dualism of unconscious nature (prakṛti) and many conscious selves (puruṣa)
The world evolves from unmanifest nature; effects pre-exist in their causes (satkāryavāda)
Liberation is kaivalya — the isolation of consciousness from nature
common_confusions: Sāṃkhya dualism pairs consciousness with nature (including mind), not mind with body as in Cartesian dualism — intellect and ego belong to prakṛti, not puruṣa.
Unlike Advaita Vedānta, Sāṃkhya affirms many real selves and a real, independent nature.
Classical Sāṃkhya is atheistic, unlike the closely allied theistic Yoga school.

sources.title: Sāṅkhya (Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Ferenc Ruzsa
sources.passage: The two types of entities of Sāṅkhya are Prakṛti and puruṣa-s, namely Nature and persons. [...] Prakṛti, or Nature, is comprised of three guṇa-s or qualities. The highest of the three is sattva (essence), the principle of light, goodness and intelligence. [...] The effect already exists in its cause prior to its production... the theory states that there is nothing absolutely new in the product. [...] The actual order of evolution is as follows: from root-nature first appears intellect (buddhi); from it, ego (ahaṁkāra); from it the eleven powers. [...] It is a strong Indian example of metaphysical dualism, but unlike many Western counterparts it is atheistic. [...] The most significant divergence is perhaps the development of a theistic school of Orthodox Hindu philosophy, called Yoga, which absorbs the basic dualism of Sāṅkhya, but is theistic, and thus regards one puruṣa as a special puruṣa, called the Lord (Īśvara). [...] The first premise of Sāṅkhya is the universal fact of suffering. [...] Sāṅkhya recognizes only three valid sources of information: perception, inference and reliable tradition. [...] (Sāṃkhyakārikā dated) ca. 350 C.E.

review.notes: Drafted for D2-001 batch 2. Source URL fetched and passages captured on 2026-07-06. Review needed: no human domain review yet; single-source card — add the Sāṃkhyakārikā as a primary source at review.
